title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: This lesson is centered on the Mangakōtukutuku Stream, a significant waterway flowing through our rohe that holds deep cultural, spiritual, and ecological importance to Ngāti Kahungunu. Students will explore the historical relationship between local hapū and the awa, examining both traditional kaitiaki practices and contemporary environmental challenges affecting its mauri.
learningObjectives: Identify and explain at least three traditional Ngāti Kahungunu practices of kaitiakitanga specific to the Mangakōtukutuku Stream.
Analyze current environmental data to assess the health of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream and identify one significant human impact.
Collaboratively develop and present a proposal for a practical kaitiaki action that addresses an identified issue affecting the awa.
Articulate the connection between the health of the awa and the cultural wellbeing of Ngāti Kahungunu, using appropriate te reo Māori terms.

activities.title: Whakapapa o Te Awa: Connecting to Our Waterway
activities.description: Students begin by listening to a local kaumātua or kaitiaki share the pūrākau (story) and significance of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream. This is followed by a guided waiata (song) that speaks of the awa, helping students to emotionally and culturally connect to the place they will be studying.

activities.title: He Rongoā, He Tikanga: Traditional Kaitiaki Practices
activities.description: In small groups, students rotate through learning stations exploring traditional practices. Stations include identifying native riparian plants (e.g., harakeke, toetoe) and their uses, learning about traditional methods for monitoring fish populations (like tuna - eels), and understanding the concept of rāhui (temporary prohibition) as a conservation tool.

activities.title: Te Arotake Taiao: Stream Health Investigation
activities.description: Using water testing kits, students conduct a hands-on ecological assessment of a safe, accessible section of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream. They measure pH, nitrate levels, clarity, and macroinvertebrate diversity, recording their data to build a snapshot of the awa's current mauri (life force).

activities.title: Ngā Pānga o Āianei: Analyzing Modern Impacts
activities.description: Students analyze their collected stream data alongside historical maps and land-use records (e.g., old farming maps, urban development plans). They work in groups to create a cause-and-effect diagram tracing how past and present human activities in the catchment area have impacted the health of the stream.

activities.title: He Mahere Kaitiaki: Designing an Action Plan
activities.description: Drawing on their new understanding, student groups design a specific, actionable kaitiaki project for the stream (e.g., a planting day for native species, a community education campaign about stormwater, a proposal for a sediment trap). They prepare a brief digital or poster presentation to 'pitch' their plan.

resources: Local Kaitiaki/Kaumātua: A knowledge holder from a local Ngāti Kahungunu marae to share pūrākau and tikanga.
Hawke's Bay Regional Council: 'State of the Environment' monitoring reports and interactive maps for the Mangakōtukutuku catchment.
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) or similar simplified water testing kits.
Digital access to the Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Incorporated website and resources on environmental policy and projects.

tasks: Submit a completed stream health data sheet with initial observations.
Create a cause-and-effect diagram analyzing one major impact on the stream.
Present a 3-minute group 'pitch' for their proposed kaitiaki action plan.

nzcAlignment: Social Studies L4: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges.
Social Studies L4: Understand how the ways in which leadership of groups is acquired and exercised have consequences for communities and societies.
Social Studies L5: Understand how people’s management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability.

The Arts L4: Understanding the Visual Arts in Context: Investigate the purpose of objects and images from past and present cultures and identify the contexts in which they were or are made, viewed, and valued.
